metadata.split_role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.

split_manifest.role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.
Expanded red/yellow, blue-staining, red-pored, and caution-lane bolete records.
Expanded Suillus and Leccinum/scaber-stalk bolete records.
Expanded long-stemmed, peppery, gilled-bolete, wood-inhabiting, and oddball bolete records.

records.notes: Cap hangs partly free from the stem rather than being attached all the way down.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Cook like other true morels after cleaning and lengthwise confirmation. Do not eat raw.
records.general_notes: Half-free morels are real morels, but their cap attachment makes beginners nervous. The hollow interior and true pitted cap are the key checks.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible after confident true-morel identification. Slice lengthwise to confirm hollow interior and cap attachment; avoid any brainy, chambered, or cottony false morels.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring morel season, usually May in Upper Michigan.

records.field_identification: Spring morel with a cap attached only partway down the stem, leaving the lower cap edge hanging free like a skirt. Cap has true pits and ridges, and the mushroom is hollow from cap tip through stem when sliced lengthwise.
records.mushroom_card_note: Spring true morel with a skirt-like half-free cap; slice lengthwise before trusting it.

use_links.label: MSU Extension: May is Morel Month
use_links.notes: Michigan-oriented morel safety and harvest context.

edible_use.notes: Good edible after confident true-morel identification. Slice lengthwise to confirm hollow interior and cap attachment; avoid any brainy, chambered, or cottony false morels.

records.look_alike_notes: Every morel must be cut lengthwise before eating; discard any specimen that is not hollow from cap tip to stem base or has a wrinkled/brain-like cap instead of true pits. Link false morels/Gyromitra and Verpa-style early false morels as primary dangers.
records.quality_notes: Excellent spring mushroom when fresh and firm; quality drops when old, dry, buggy, sandy, or mushy.
records.eating_use_notes: Clean well, cut lengthwise, inspect each specimen, and cook thoroughly. Try a small first serving.
records.risk_reason: False morels and Verpa-type look-alikes can be poisonous. Even true morels can cause gastrointestinal upset if raw, undercooked, eaten in large amounts, or possibly combined with alcohol.
records.symptoms_body_systems: If confused with false morels: gastrointestinal tract, liver, kidneys, nervous system, and blood. If true morels are raw/undercooked: gastrointestinal upset.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Morel-like mushrooms may be wrinkled, lobed, chambered, or attached differently. True morels should be hollow from cap tip through stem when cut lengthwise, with the cap properly attached to the stem.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat morels raw or undercooked. Do not eat any “morel” unless every specimen is cut lengthwise and confirmed as true Morchella.
records.caution_reason: False morels and Verpa-type look-alikes can be poisonous. Even true morels can cause gastrointestinal upset if raw, undercooked, eaten in large amounts, or possibly combined with alcohol.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — false morel / Verpa confusion possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: Extreme — false morel / Verpa confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Half-free morels require a special attachment check because the lower cap edge hangs free, but the cap is not attached only at the top like Verpa.
required_checks: Cut every specimen lengthwise
Interior must be hollow from cap through stem
Cap attached about halfway down the stem
Reject top-only attached caps, cottony interiors, chambered interiors, brain-like folds, saddle shapes, or non-hollow specimens
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Half-free morels must be hollow and attached partway down the stem; top-only attached caps are Verpa caution.

target_traits: Cap attachment matches true morel type
lookalike_traits: Brain-like, wrinkled, lobed, or saddle-like cap
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Non-hollow or brain-like morel-ish mushrooms are not edible morels.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Not edible / early false morel caution.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Top-attached free-hanging cap means Verpa caution, not true morel.

records.notes: Instead of gills or pores, these have short dangling teeth under the cap.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Trim dirty tooth layer if needed. Good sautéed or added to mixed mushroom dishes; texture stays firmer than many gilled mushrooms.
records.general_notes: One of the friendlier edible mushrooms for beginners because true teeth are easier to read than subtle gill attachment.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when young and confidently identified by the tooth underside. Older specimens can become bitter or buggy.

records.field_identification: Pale buff to orange-tan cap with blunt, removable teeth under the cap instead of gills or pores. Teeth run slightly down the stem; flesh is firm and brittle. The tooth underside is the deal-breaker ID feature.
records.mushroom_card_note: Look underneath: blunt hanging teeth, not gills. Choice edible when young.

edible_use.notes: Choice edible when young and confidently identified by the tooth underside. Older specimens can become bitter or buggy.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — tooth/gill/quality confusion
lookalike_risk.summary: Hedgehogs are comparatively safe when the underside has soft teeth/spines, but underside confirmation is mandatory.
required_checks: Underside teeth required
Habitat/substrate check required
Reject ridges, gills, or dark scaly toothed mushrooms

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: No teeth = not a hedgehog.
Wood-growing tooth mass routes to Hericium, not Hydnum.

target_traits: Pale peach/tan Hydnum with soft teeth
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Dark scaly toothed mushrooms are not beginner hedgehogs.

records.medicinal_uses: Traditional reishi-style use; this guide should present this as traditional/field-guide use rather than medical treatment.
records.culinary_uses: Not eaten as a mushroom. Usually dried for tea/tincture by people who already know what they are doing.
records.edibility_detail: Not a food mushroom. Used in some traditional/reishi-style preparations, but this guide treats it as a cautious medicinal/practical ID entry, not a meal.
records.general_notes: Host matters: Ganoderma tsugae is strongly associated with hemlock/conifer wood, separating it from some hardwood Ganoderma look-alikes.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Annual bracket; most noticeable summer into fall.

records.field_identification: Glossy varnished red to reddish-brown shelf or kidney-shaped bracket on hemlock or other conifer wood. White pore surface bruises brown; flesh is corky/woody, not a tender eating mushroom.
records.mushroom_card_note: Glossy red varnished shelf on hemlock; not food, traditional-use only.

edible_use.notes: Not a food mushroom. Used in some traditional/reishi-style preparations, but this guide treats it as a cautious medicinal/practical ID entry, not a meal.

records.notes: Large clustered rosette of overlapping fronds at the base of oaks and other hardwoods.
records.medicinal_uses: Maitake has supplement interest, but this guide treats wild hens mainly as a prime food mushroom.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible. Trim away tough bases, clean the layered fronds well, and cook; useful sautéed, roasted, dried, or in soups and gravies.
records.general_notes: A good host-linked species and one of the better fall edible clusters.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when fresh, young, and confidently identified; older rosettes get tough or bug-ridden.

staining: Blackens when old or bruised slightly
mushroom_profile.season_note: Mostly fall, especially at oak bases.

records.field_identification: Large gray-brown rosette of many overlapping spoon- or fan-shaped fronds, usually at the base of oak or other hardwoods. The underside has pores, not gills, and the fruitbody arises from a branching base.
records.mushroom_card_note: Choice fall edible at oak bases; look for a big layered rosette with pored undersides.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Grifola frondosa
use_links.notes: Hen-of-the-woods rosette form, pored underside, hardwood-base habitat, and comparison notes.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — polypore/quality confusion
lookalike_risk.summary: No major deadly look-alike is flagged, but hen can be confused with other rosette polypores and quality declines with age.
required_checks: Substrate/tree-base check required
Pored underside required
No gills
Reject black-staining or thick cream shelf forms as hen
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Black-staining fronds, thick cream shelves, or ribbon folds are not hen of the woods.

target_traits: Gray-brown rosette of many spoon/fan fronds at oak base
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Black-staining fronds are not hen of the woods.
Thick cream shelves are not maitake.

lookalike_traits: Individual funnel-like caps on branching stalks
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Funnel caps on branching stalks key to umbrella polypore, not hen.
Ribbon folds are not hen.

records.notes: Clustered gilled mushrooms on wood and roots.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Cook completely; many foragers parboil/blanch first. Start with a small serving. Caution is due to look-alikes and digestibility, not poor food value.
records.edibility_detail: Edible with caution for experienced foragers only. Confirm a white spore print, wood/root growth, pale attached-to-decurrent gills, and Armillaria traits. Cook thoroughly; avoid old specimens and try a small amount first.
records.general_notes: Honey mushrooms are a species complex. The combination of clustered growth from wood/roots, white spores, ring/rhizomorph clues, and lack of orange glow helps separate them from jack-o-lanterns.

records.field_identification: Dense clusters on wood, buried roots, or at tree bases; tan to honey-brown caps often with small scales; white spore print; attached to slightly decurrent pale gills; many have a ring on the stem and dark shoestring-like rhizomorphs near the wood.
records.mushroom_card_note: Clustered wood/root mushroom with white spores; caution lane, not beginner food.

edible_use.notes: Cook completely and start with a small serving. Many foragers parboil or blanch first. This is an edible-with-caution record because of toxic look-alikes and individual GI reactions, not because the food value is automatically poor.

records.look_alike_notes: Honey mushrooms have white spores and Armillaria-style growth on wood, roots, or buried wood. Deadly galerina has rusty-brown spores and small brown caps; sulfur tuft has yellow-green gills and purple-brown spores; brick cap has gray to purple-gray gills and brick-red caps; shaggy scalycap has heavy scales and cinnamon-brown spores; jack-o-lanterns are orange, clustered on wood, and poisonous. Do not eat honey mushrooms unless a mature cap gives a white spore print; deadly galerina can grow on the same wood and has a rusty-brown spore print, while jack-o’-lanterns can share the white spore print but have bright orange flesh and true orange gills.
records.foraging_value: Fair to good edible for experienced foragers only
records.caution_reason: Look-alike risk with deadly Galerina and jack-o’-lantern, plus real GI intolerance in some people.
records.flavor_texture_notes: Best young and firm; older caps and stems can be tough, buggy, or trigger individual reactions.
records.quality_notes: Rich, slightly sweet, sometimes mucilaginous; young tight caps are best.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook completely; many foragers parboil or blanch first. Start with a small serving.
records.risk_reason: Honey mushrooms overlap in habitat and season with deadly Galerina and poisonous jack-o’-lanterns. Correctly identified honey mushrooms also bother some stomachs.
records.symptoms_body_systems: If honey mushroom intolerance: gastrointestinal upset. If confused with Galerina: delayed amatoxin poisoning affecting liver and kidneys. If confused with jack-o’-lantern: cramps, vomiting, and diarrhea.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Wood-associated growth, tan/yellow-brown caps, clustered fruiting, variable ring presence, and novice confusion with other wood-growing mushrooms. White spore print supports honey mushroom; rusty-brown spore print points away from honey and toward Galerina danger.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat honey mushrooms without a white spore print and positive Armillaria/Desarmillaria identification. Any rusty-brown spore print, sharp orange gills, or uncertainty means do not eat.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — deadly wood-mushroom confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Honey mushrooms can be good food for experienced foragers, but Deadly Galerina and other clustered wood mushrooms are similar enough that spore print and specimen-by-specimen checks are mandatory.
required_checks: White spore print required
Confirm Armillaria/Desarmillaria traits
Check every specimen in mixed wood clusters
Reject rusty-brown, cinnamon, purple-brown, greenish, or dark spores
Reject bright pumpkin-orange flesh or sharp true gills
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Treat every honey mushroom collection specimen-by-specimen. One wrong wood mushroom ruins the basket.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly — amatoxin poisoning can damage liver and kidneys after delayed symptoms.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly Galerina can grow on wood near honey mushrooms; spore print color is a stop-sign check.
target_traits: Armillaria/Desarmillaria growth from wood, roots, or buried wood
Each specimen in a cluster matches the honey-mushroom group being used
lookalike_traits: Thin ring or ring zone may be present
Can grow mixed with other wood mushrooms
required_checks: Take a spore print from mature caps
Check every specimen in a mixed wood cluster
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Any rusty-brown spore print means do not eat.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Jack-o’-lanterns can overlap with clustered wood-growing mushrooms and are poisonous.
target_traits: Honey-yellow to brown tones rather than bright pumpkin-orange flesh
Honey-mushroom structure, not thick orange true gills
lookalike_traits: Dense clusters from wood, stumps, or buried roots
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not eat orange clustered wood mushrooms as honey mushrooms.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not edible — clustered wood mushroom with dark spores.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Sulfur tuft is a clustered wood mushroom; yellow-green gills and purple-brown spores separate it from honey mushrooms.
target_traits: Honey-brown cap colors without greenish gills
lookalike_traits: Gills become greenish to dark purple-brown
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Greenish gills or purple-brown spores mean do not eat.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Unsafe / not recommended — Pholiota-type clustered wood mushroom.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Shaggy scalycap is not a deadly twin like Galerina, but it is a common honey-mushroom confusion trap.
target_traits: Honey-mushroom cap and stem without coarse shaggy scales covering both
Armillaria/Desarmillaria traits match consistently across the collection
required_checks: Check cap/stem texture
Take a spore print when wood-cluster ID is uncertain
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not eat mixed wood clusters unless each mushroom is separated and confirmed.

records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Wood-growing orange clusters with sharp true gills are not chanterelles.
records.general_notes: This should stay in the caution side of the app. It is a key chanterelle look-alike and belongs in the gilled lane, not 'other'.
search_aliases: Omphalotus illudens, the eastern North American jack-o’-lantern mushroom.

substrate: hardwood stumps, dead wood, dying trees, buried hardwood roots
mushroom_profile.spore_print: white to creamy or pale yellow
growth_form: occasionally smaller clusters from buried roots
mushroom_profile.gill_attachment: decurrent; gills run down the stem

records.scientific_name: Omphalotus illudens, the eastern North American jack-o’-lantern mushroom.
records.poisoning_effects: Painful stomach cramps, vomiting, and diarrhea.
records.field_identification: Bright orange to pumpkin-orange mushroom, usually in clusters. Grows from hardwood stumps, buried roots, or dead/dying hardwood wood; can appear to grow from the ground when attached to buried wood. True gills are sharp-edged, close/crowded, orange, and run down the stem. Flesh is orange or orangish when sliced. Spore print is white to creamy or pale yellow. Fresh gills can glow faintly green in the dark, but glow is not reliable enough to use as the main ID trait.
records.overview: Searchable danger entry for Jack-o’-Lantern. Included because it can be confused with edible mushrooms in Upper Michigan / northern Great Lakes foraging contexts.
records.look_alike_notes: Chanterelles: Chanterelles grow singly or scattered from the forest floor and have blunt false gills/ridges, not sharp true gills. Jack-o’-lanterns usually cluster on wood or buried roots. False Chanterelle: False chanterelle is also orange with true gill-like structures but is generally smaller/flimsier and not the same clustered wood-rotting jack-o’-lantern hazard.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Omphalotus illudens
use_links.notes: Clustered wood growth, orange color, true gills, and chanterelle comparison.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Omphalotus illudens
use_links.notes: field identification, spore print, substrate, chanterelle look-alike notes

use_links.label: Cornell Mushroom Blog: An adventure with Omphalotus
use_links.notes: chanterelle confusion, symptoms, wood vs ground distinction

use_links.label: Annals of Emergency Medicine / PubMed: Jack-o’-lantern mushroom poisoning

records.mushroom_card_note: Do not eat orange clustered mushrooms from wood or buried roots. True gills plus orange clusters equals no, even if it looks chanterelle-ish.
records.toxicity_notes: Poisonous gastrointestinal irritant; commonly confused with chanterelles.
records.risk_reason: Commonly mistaken for chanterelles because of the orange/yellow-orange color and decurrent gill-like underside.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract; painful stomach cramps, vomiting, and diarrhea.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Grows in clusters from rotting wood, stumps, or buried roots and has true, sharp-edged gills. Chanterelles grow from soil and have blunt forked ridges/false gills.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat orange “chanterelles” growing in clusters from wood or with sharp true gills.
records.caution_reason: Poisonous chanterelle look-alike; wood growth and true gills are stop signs.

records.notes: King Bolete / porcini is kept as a practical field-guide page, but North American Boletus edulis-like mushrooms are taxonomically messy. Treat this as an edulis-group/porcini-style entry unless a collection is confirmed to species level.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent sautéed, dried, or used in soups and sauces when young and firm. Avoid soft, old, bug-ridden specimens.
records.general_notes: Use as a group record for porcini/king bolete types in the region; do not treat every brown-capped bolete as king bolete.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible for experienced bolete foragers after confirming white-to-yellow pores, no blue staining, no red pores, and no bitter taste clue. North American taxonomy is messy, so treat this as the Boletus edulis group.
records.scientific_name: Boletus edulis group / North American porcini complex
records.duplicate_note: Duplicate King Bolete record kept only as a legacy slug alias.

images_structured.title: King bolete field view
images_structured.author: George Chernilevsky
images_structured.rights_notes: Exact author/license metadata copied from local credit table (credits-enrichment-v70); source page retained for attribution.

records.foraging_value: Prime foraging — species group needs care
records.mushroom_card_note: Porcini lane: white flesh, no blue staining, no red pores, reticulate upper stem.
records.field_identification: Stout bolete with a brown bun-like cap, white pores when young becoming yellow-olive with age, thick pale stem often with fine white netting near the top, and firm white flesh that does not blue-stain.

edible_use.notes: Choice edible for experienced bolete foragers after confirming white-to-yellow pores, no blue staining, no red pores, and no bitter taste clue. North American taxonomy is messy, so treat this as the Boletus edulis group.

records.quality_notes: Excellent when young and firm; quality drops fast when soft, old, buggy, or waterlogged.
records.eating_use_notes: Excellent sautéed, dried, or used in soups and sauces. Use only firm, clean specimens.
records.risk_reason: Some boletes cause severe GI reactions, and edible boletes can be confused with toxic, bitter, or poorly tolerated species.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract; severe nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, weakness, and dehydration are possible with toxic/problem boletes.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Pores instead of gills can make beginners overconfident. Risk traits include red/orange pores, rapid blue staining, bitter taste, unusual staining, and lack of clear porcini traits.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat unknown boletes. Avoid red-pored boletes, bitter boletes, and rapidly blue-staining boletes unless identified to species by an expert.
records.caution_reason: Bolete ID still matters; “has pores” does not make it food.

lookalike_risk.label: High — poisonous/severe-GI and bitter bolete confusion possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — poisonous/severe-GI and bitter bolete confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Porcini-type boletes are food only when bitter Tylopilus, false king bolete, and red-pored/fast-blue-staining boletes are ruled out.
required_checks: Confirm non-blue-staining white flesh
Confirm white young pores aging yellow/olive
Confirm pale netting near upper stem when expected
Reject pinkish pores plus bitter taste
Reject red/orange pores
Reject strong or inconsistent blue/green-blue staining
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat any bolete that does not match the full porcini package.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: False king bolete is a northern/Great Lakes porcini-type danger concern.
target_traits: Pale netting near upper stem when expected
lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If false king traits are possible, do not eat.

target_traits: White/yellow pores without bitter Tylopilus posture
required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Detailed culinary and caution notes are still being copied back.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually encountered from September through November in the Upper Midwest / Great Lakes window.

records.notes: Larch Suillus is treated here as Suillus clintonianus, the North American larch/tamarack-associated species often shown in older guides as Suillus grevillei. Check for larch/tamarack nearby.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily culinary/ID interest in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Edible when young and carefully prepared, but sticky cap skin and soft texture make it less desirable than prime boletes. Best cooked; peel slimy cap skin if objectionable.
records.edibility_detail: Treat as caution / not for meals in this guide until species-level edibility is clarified.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Larch Suillus.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually encountered from July through September in the Upper Midwest / Great Lakes window.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Suillus clintonianus
use_links.notes: North American larch/tamarack Suillus; separated from European Suillus grevillei.

records.field_identification: Sticky orange-yellow to reddish Suillus associated with larch/tamarack; check for nearby larch and a slimy cap.
records.mushroom_card_note: Fair Suillus tied to larch/tamarack; edible but not a prime bolete.

records.medicinal_uses: Popular medicinal-interest species, especially for nerve/cognition claims, but this guide should treat claims as preliminary/traditional unless supported by specific research.
records.culinary_uses: Tear or slice fresh white flesh and cook thoroughly; often used like seafood-textured mushroom in sautés or patties.
records.general_notes: Separate from bear’s head tooth and coral tooth by growth form: lion’s mane is mostly unbranched with one compact mass of teeth.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when fresh, white, and confidently identified. Avoid yellowed, soggy, or old specimens.
records.field_identification: Single unbranched white pom-pom or beard of long dangling teeth, usually from a wound on living hardwood or from recently dead hardwood. It lacks the branched coral-like structure of other Hericium species.
records.mushroom_card_note: White unbranched pom-pom of dangling teeth on hardwood; choice food, medicinal-interest claims stay cautious.

images_structured.author: George Chernilevsky

edible_use.notes: Choice edible when fresh, white, and confidently identified. Avoid yellowed, soggy, or old specimens.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — edible Hericium confusion / condition risk
lookalike_risk.short_label: Moderate — edible Hericium confusion / condition risk
lookalike_risk.summary: Hericium risk is mostly edible species confusion and condition: verify white toothed growth on hardwood and reject yellowed/soggy/old material.
required_checks: Confirm white soft toothed fungus
Confirm wood/hardwood growth
Check branching/tooth arrangement if species-level matters
Reject yellowed, brown, soggy, or old specimens
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: No teeth, no Hericium; use Hericium group-level if species separation is uncertain.

target_traits: Wood-growing Hericium mass with hanging teeth
lookalike_traits: Terrestrial cap-and-stem mushroom with teeth under a cap
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Cap-and-stem teeth on soil = Hydnum-style hedgehog, not Hericium.

lookalike_traits: Upright coral branches without hanging teeth
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: No teeth, no Hericium.

records.notes: A parasitized mushroom turned bright orange-red and dense, usually from a russula or milkcap host.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Detailed culinary and caution notes are still being copied back.
records.general_notes: Best learned as a transformation fungus rather than a normal cap-and-gill mushroom.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when fresh, firm, and fully transformed. This is a parasitic fungus on other mushrooms, usually Russula or Lactarius. Eat only firm, fresh, fully orange specimens with the host mushroom clearly transformed.

smell: mild to seafood-like in some specimens

images_structured.title: Lobster mushroom cross-section and parasite surface detail
images_structured.author: Jason Hollinger

records.foraging_value: Choice edible when fresh, firm, and fully transformed
records.field_identification: A bright orange to reddish-orange parasitic fungus that covers and deforms a host mushroom, usually a white Russula or Lactarius. Mature lobster mushrooms are firm, dense, irregularly shaped, and covered with a hard, warty or pimply orange crust. The original host’s gills are usually reduced, blunted, or completely obscured.
records.look_alike_notes: Few true look-alikes when fully developed, but partial infections, rotten specimens, and orange moldy mushrooms should not be treated as lobster mushrooms. The whole mushroom should be firm and thoroughly transformed, not just patched with orange mold. Do not eat specimens where the host is still mostly visible, slimy, decaying, or foul-smelling.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Reject soft, slimy, waterlogged, moldy, sour, insect-riddled, faded, brown, mushy, or foul-smelling specimens. Reject partially parasitized mushrooms where normal gills and host traits remain obvious. Slice open before cooking; interior should be firm and clean, not rotten.
records.image_needs: Needs whole bright orange specimen, warty/pimply crust close-up, obscured/deformed gills, cut pale firm interior, partially infected/reject specimen, and hardwood habitat image.
records.quality_notes: Dense, seafood-like aroma; old specimens become fishy, light, purple, soft, or foul.
records.eating_use_notes: Trim dirty folds, cook well, and use only firm fully parasitized specimens. Dry/powder only sound specimens that are not spoiled.
records.risk_reason: Risk is incomplete parasitization, age/condition, and uncertainty of the host. Half-parasitized forms are not app-safe food candidates.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Mostly gastrointestinal upset risk from spoiled, old, partially parasitized, or uncertain specimens.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Orange crust should thoroughly cover/deform the host mushroom. Reject specimens where the host is still mostly visible, soft, slimy, purple, foul-smelling, or only patched with orange mold.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat partial, rotten, soft, foul, or mostly untransformed orange-moldy mushrooms as lobster mushrooms.
records.caution_reason: Condition and complete parasitization matter more than the name.

lookalike_risk.label: High — host/partial-infection uncertainty
lookalike_risk.summary: Lobster mushroom is a parasitized Russula/Lactarius-type host. Risk rises with partial infection, rot, mold, or orange-gilled look-alikes.
required_checks: Cut/check condition required
Host/shape check required
Reject partial orange coating if host traits remain unclear
Reject old, foul, light, purple, soft, or moldy specimens
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Partial orange coating is not enough; if normal host traits are still doing the talking, identify the host separately.

target_traits: Fully transformed hard orange parasite crust
lookalike_traits: White Russula host not fully parasitized
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Partial orange coating is not enough.

lookalike_traits: Milkcap traits still visible, latex present, incomplete parasitism
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If milkcap traits remain, identify the host separately.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Orange true-gilled clusters from wood are not the same as edible shelf/ridge mushrooms.
target_traits: Target mushroom lacks true orange gills
lookalike_traits: Dense clusters from wood, roots, or buried roots
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If it has true orange gills, route away from the edible target.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Cook young, clean specimens only after confident ID. Discard anything with chemical/phenolic smell, strong yellow staining, or uncertain base.
records.edibility_detail: Edible only after careful Agaricus identification. Beginners should avoid any white mushroom until they can rule out Amanita: check gill color, spore print, stem base, ring, bruising, and odor.
records.general_notes: This overlaps the field mushroom idea; keep the deadly Amanita contrast visible because white lawn mushrooms are a common mistake zone.

staining: May bruise slightly pinkish; avoid strong yellow staining

records.field_identification: Grassland Agaricus with pink young gills that turn chocolate brown, free gills, no volva/cup at the base, and a brown spore print. It should not have white mature gills, a bulbous cup, or a chemical yellow-staining smell.
records.mushroom_card_note: Pink-to-brown gills and brown spore print are required; white gills/cup at base = stop.

edible_use.notes: Cook only after careful Agaricus identification. Confirm pink-to-chocolate gills, brown spore print, no volva, no bright yellow base staining, and no phenolic/ink-like odor.

records.caution_reason: Yellow-staining/phenolic Agaricus can cause GI poisoning, and deadly Amanitas can be confused with white lawn mushrooms if gills, spores, base, ring, odor, and staining are not checked.
records.quality_notes: Store-mushroom-like food value when the species is good; older specimens get dark, buggy, or unpleasant.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook and require spore print, odor, staining, habitat, and species-complex confirmation.
records.risk_reason: Yellow-staining/phenolic Agaricus can cause GI poisoning, and deadly Amanitas can be confused with white lawn mushrooms if gills, spores, base, ring, odor, and staining are not checked.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Yellow-staining Agaricus: gastrointestinal tract with nausea, cramps, diarrhea, and vomiting. Amanita confusion: liver and kidney failure risk.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Free gills that start pink and become chocolate-brown, brown spore print, ring, no volva, staining reaction, and odor all matter. Bright yellow staining at the stem base or phenolic/ink/iodine smell is a stop sign.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat Agaricus with bright yellow staining at the stem base or a chemical/phenolic odor. Do not eat white-gilled mushrooms unless Amanita has been ruled out by digging up the entire base.
records.alias_note: Field mushroom is treated here as the same practical campestris-like grassland Agaricus concept as meadow mushroom.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — deadly Amanita-button confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Meadow/field Agaricus can be good food, but button-stage white mushrooms require gill/spore/base checks, and yellow-staining Agaricus plus green-spored parasols are serious traps.
required_checks: Confirm gills mature pink to chocolate-brown
Dark brown spore print required when uncertain
No volva, cup, sack, or bulb at the base
Reject bright yellow staining at stem base
Reject phenolic/inky/iodine/chemical odor
Reject parasol-like mushrooms with greenish gills or dull green spore print
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat Agaricus buttons until gill color, spore print, stem base, staining, and odor all fit.

required_checks: Dig up entire base
Spore print required if uncertain
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: White gills/spores plus any volva/cup/bulb means do not eat.

target_traits: No strong chrome-yellow staining at stem base
lookalike_traits: Bright yellow staining at base/cap edge
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bright yellow base staining or chemical odor means do not eat.

required_checks: Spore print required if parasol-like or uncertain
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Green spore print means do not eat as Agaricus.
